@@ -15,7 +15,7 @@ {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-17e.2","title":"Set up GitHub Actions for installer testing","description":"# Task: Set up GitHub Actions for Installer Testing ## Description Create GitHub Actions workflow that tests the installer on real Ubuntu. ## Workflow File `.github/workflows/installer.yml` ## Jobs ### 1. shellcheck Fast job that runs on every PR: ```yaml - uses: ludeeus/action-shellcheck@master with: scandir: './scripts' additional_files: 'install.sh' ``` ### 2. test-ubuntu-25 Full installer test (may be slow): ```yaml runs-on: ubuntu-latest container: image: ubuntu:25.04 steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Run installer run: | bash install.sh --yes --mode vibe - name: Run doctor run: | su - ubuntu -c "acfs doctor" - name: Verify key tools run: | su - ubuntu -c "ntm --help" su - ubuntu -c "ubs --help" # ... etc ``` ### 3. test-ubuntu-24 Same but on 24.04 LTS for compatibility. ## Triggers - Push to main - Pull requests touching install.sh or scripts/ ## Matrix Testing ```yaml strategy: matrix: ubuntu: ['24.04', '25.04'] ``` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] shellcheck passes on all scripts - [ ] Installer works on Ubuntu 24.04 - [ ] Installer works on Ubuntu 25.04 - [ ] All tools verified after install - [ ] acfs doctor exits 0","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-19T22:27:21.687302-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T12:46:57.410761-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T12:46:57.410761-05:00","close_reason":"Closed","labels":["ci","task"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-17e.2","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-17e","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-19T22:27:21.688994-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-187","title":"[cleanup] Consolidate duplicate acfs_resolve_selection implementations","description":"## Issue There are TWO implementations of `acfs_resolve_selection`: 1. **scripts/lib/install_helpers.sh** (lines 43-272) - Actively used by install.sh (sourced via detect_environment) - More comprehensive: has SKIP_TAGS, SKIP_CATEGORIES, ACFS_PLAN_REASON, ACFS_PLAN_EXCLUDE_REASON 2. **scripts/lib/selection.sh** (lines 195-315) - Has test file (test_selection.sh) with 10 passing tests - NOT sourced anywhere in install.sh - effectively dead code - Cleaner separation with named helper functions ## Problem - Duplicate code creates maintenance burden - Tests exist for selection.sh but production uses install_helpers.sh - If someone modifies selection.sh thinking it's the canonical implementation, those changes won't take effect ## Options 1. **Delete selection.sh**: Remove the unused file and its tests, keep install_helpers.sh 2. **Migrate to selection.sh**: Move selection logic to selection.sh, source it from install_helpers.sh 3. **Merge best of both**: Combine the clean structure of selection.sh with the features of install_helpers.sh ## Recommendation Option 3: selection.sh has better test coverage and cleaner code structure. Merge the extra features from install_helpers.sh (SKIP_TAGS, SKIP_CATEGORIES, reason tracking) into selection.sh, then source selection.sh from install_helpers.sh. ## Files Involved - scripts/lib/selection.sh (standalone, tested, unused) - scripts/lib/test_selection.sh (tests selection.sh) - scripts/lib/install_helpers.sh (has duplicate implementation, used) ## Priority P3 - Not urgent, code works, but should be cleaned up to avoid confusion.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:35:13.451373-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:49:28.168892-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:49:28.168892-05:00","close_reason":"Removed duplicate selection.sh. Tests now verify install_helpers.sh (production). All 10 tests pass."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-19d4","title":"[EPIC] VPS Conceptual Framework","description":"# Epic: VPS Conceptual Framework ## Problem Statement Users don't understand what a VPS is, why they need one, or how it differs from their laptop. This creates: 1. Hesitation ("why can't I just run this on my laptop?") 2. Confusion about provider account vs VPS instance 3. Anxiety about ongoing costs (note: costs ARE explained, but "why pay at all?" isn't) 4. Mental model gaps that cause problems later ## Background & Reasoning The VPS is the "engine room" metaphor - but we introduce this metaphor AFTER they've already committed to renting one. By then, they've either proceeded on faith or dropped off. Beginners need to understand: - VPS = a computer in a datacenter that runs 24/7 - Your laptop = the remote control - Why this matters: persistent sessions, agents keep running, work from any device - Account vs Instance: signing up for Hetzner ≠ having a running server The region selection and RAM requirements are also opaque. "Pick a region close to you" - why? "16GB RAM" - what does that mean in practical terms? ## User Story As someone considering ACFS, I want to understand why I need a VPS and what it does, So that I can make an informed decision and understand the architecture. ## Success Criteria 1. User understands the laptop ↔ VPS relationship before committing 2. User understands persistence benefit (work continues when laptop closes) 3. User understands provider account vs VPS instance distinction 4. User understands region selection (closer = faster) 5. User understands RAM requirements (more RAM = more agents) ## Scope - Add "Why VPS?" section early in wizard (before rent-vps step) - Clarify account vs instance in rent-vps → create-vps transition - Improve region and RAM explanations with practical context - Use the "engine room" metaphor earlier and consistently ## Dependencies None - can be developed in parallel with Terminal epic ## UI/UX Requirements - Maintain current polish for desktop and mobile - Consider an animated diagram showing laptop → internet → VPS - Use Jargon component for technical terms - Ensure explanations don't make the wizard feel longer (progressive disclosure) ## Technical Approach 1. Add "Why VPS?" content to rent-vps page or a new interstitial 2. Enhance create-vps page with better RAM/region explanations 3. Add transition text between rent-vps and create-vps clarifying the distinction 4. Use GuideExplain components for the conceptual content ## Estimated Effort Medium (2 wizard page updates, new conceptual content) ## Priority Justification P1 (High) - Conceptual understanding prevents drop-off and reduces support burden.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:34:28.001115-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T13:34:28.001115-05:00"} -{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-1j8r","title":"Add key auth vs password explanation for reconnection","description":"# Task: Add Key Auth vs Password Explanation ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Post-Install Transition Experience (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-r2np) ## Description The reconnect command uses `-i ~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519` which is NEW. Users don't understand they're now using key authentication instead of password. They might wonder "where do I enter my password?" or panic when they're not prompted for one. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/reconnect-ubuntu/page.tsx Add explanation above the SSH command: \`\`\`tsx

Notice something different?

This SSH command uses your SSH key (the -i ~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519 part) instead of a password. The installer set this up for you!

No password needed! When you run this command, you'll connect immediately without typing a password. That's the magic of SSH keys — more secure AND more convenient.

\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Explains the change from password to key auth - [ ] Reassures that no password prompt is expected - [ ] Briefly mentions this is more secure - [ ] Appears before the SSH command - [ ] Friendly, not technical tone ## UI/UX Notes - Frame it as a positive upgrade, not a change to learn - "Magic" language makes it feel like a benefit - Keep it brief — users are eager to reconnect","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:42:42.043314-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:19:45.450821-05:00"} +{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-1j8r","title":"Add key auth vs password explanation for reconnection","description":"# Task: Add Key Auth vs Password Explanation ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Post-Install Transition Experience (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-r2np) ## Description The reconnect command uses `-i ~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519` which is NEW. Users don't understand they're now using key authentication instead of password. They might wonder "where do I enter my password?" or panic when they're not prompted for one. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/reconnect-ubuntu/page.tsx Add explanation above the SSH command: \`\`\`tsx

Notice something different?

This SSH command uses your SSH key (the -i ~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519 part) instead of a password. The installer set this up for you!

No password needed! When you run this command, you'll connect immediately without typing a password. That's the magic of SSH keys — more secure AND more convenient.

\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Explains the change from password to key auth - [ ] Reassures that no password prompt is expected - [ ] Briefly mentions this is more secure - [ ] Appears before the SSH command - [ ] Friendly, not technical tone ## UI/UX Notes - Frame it as a positive upgrade, not a change to learn - "Magic" language makes it feel like a benefit - Keep it brief — users are eager to reconnect","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:42:42.043314-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:28:00.229279-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:28:00.229279-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented key-auth (no password) explanation above reconnect SSH command"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-1of","title":"[codex.5] Update README codex references","description":"## Goal Audit and fix any codex-cli authentication references in README.md. ## Scope 1. Search README.md for any mention of: - OPENAI_API_KEY - codex authentication - agent login 2. Update any incorrect guidance 3. Add correct auth flow description if missing ## Correct Information - codex-cli uses OAuth via ChatGPT Pro/Plus account - Authentication command: codex auth - No API key needed - Browser-based login flow ## Files to Modify - README.md ## Acceptance Criteria 1. No incorrect OPENAI_API_KEY references for codex 2. Clear, correct auth guidance if discussed","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:26:01.346705-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:43:44.97006-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:43:44.97006-05:00","close_reason":"Fixed README.md codex auth references: API key -> OAuth, set OPENAI_API_KEY -> run codex login","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-1of","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ua5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:26:36.402862-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-1u8e","title":"Add tmux/ntm conceptual explanation","description":"# Task: Add tmux/ntm Conceptual Explanation ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Daily Workflow & Getting Started Guide (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-2cvc) ## Description Users don't understand what tmux is, why they need it, or what ntm does. They see `ntm new myproject` without understanding the underlying concept of persistent sessions. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/launch-onboarding/page.tsx or /learn/sessions Add GuideExplain component: \`\`\`tsx

The problem: When you SSH into your VPS and then close your laptop or lose internet, your terminal session dies. Any running commands stop.

The solution: tmux creates "sessions" that keep running on the VPS even when you disconnect. Think of it like leaving a TV playing in another room — it keeps going whether you're watching or not.

NTM (Named Tmux Manager) makes tmux easier. Instead of cryptic commands, you get simple ones:

  • ntm new myproject — Start a new session
  • ntm attach myproject — Resume a session
  • ntm list — See all your sessions

This is why you can start a Claude task, close your laptop, go to bed, and come back to find it completed. The session keeps running on the VPS.

\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Explains WHY sessions matter (disconnect survival) - [ ] Uses relatable analogy (TV playing in another room) - [ ] Introduces ntm as the simple interface - [ ] Shows 3 core commands - [ ] Explains the "overnight task" use case ## UI/UX Notes - This is conceptual, not just a command reference - The "overnight task" example is compelling - Keep it scannable with short paragraphs - Works well in SimplerGuide or as standalone","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:44:24.594424-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:25:08.021828-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:25:08.021828-05:00","close_reason":"GuideExplain 'What is tmux and ntm?' implemented with: problem/solution structure, TV analogy, 3 core commands (new/attach/list), and overnight task use case. All acceptance criteria met."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-1wk6","title":"[EPIC] SSH Key Generation Clarity","description":"# Epic: SSH Key Generation Clarity ## Problem Statement SSH key generation presents three critical confusion points that cause beginners to fail or create problematic configurations: 1. "Enter file in which to save the key" - They don't know to just press Enter 2. "Enter passphrase" - They think they MUST set one, then forget it 3. No feedback that it actually worked ## Background & Reasoning SSH keys are conceptually difficult for beginners ("like a password but safer" only goes so far). The ssh-keygen prompts are designed for sysadmins, not beginners. Every prompt causes anxiety and potential wrong decisions. The passphrase issue is particularly insidious: if they set one, they'll be asked for it on EVERY SSH connection. When they inevitably forget it, they're stuck and have to regenerate keys. The ~/.ssh path is also completely foreign - users don't know what ~ means or that .ssh is a hidden folder. ## User Story As a beginner generating my first SSH key, I want clear guidance on exactly what to do at each prompt, So that I create a working key without accidentally locking myself out. ## Success Criteria 1. User understands to press Enter at file location prompt 2. User understands to press Enter TWICE at passphrase prompts (empty passphrase) 3. User sees the randomart image and knows it means success 4. User understands where their key is stored (~/.ssh explained) 5. User understands they have TWO files (public and private) and which is which ## Scope - Expand the "Generate SSH Key" wizard step - Add step-by-step guidance for each prompt - Add "What you'll see" preview for each prompt - Add success verification - Explain ~/.ssh and the two key files ## Dependencies - Epic A: Terminal Onboarding Foundation (user must understand terminal first) ## UI/UX Requirements - Maintain current polish level for both desktop and mobile - Use SimplerGuide component for detailed explanations - Consider animated GIF or video showing the process - Ensure command cards work well on mobile (copy button accessible) ## Technical Approach Enhance apps/web/app/wizard/generate-ssh-key/page.tsx: - Add GuideStep components for each prompt stage - Add OutputPreview components showing expected prompts - Add GuideCaution for passphrase warning - Add GuideTip for success verification ## Estimated Effort Small-Medium (1 wizard page enhancement) ## Priority Justification P1 (High) - Key generation is a critical gate. Wrong passphrase choice creates persistent problems.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:33:56.31213-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T13:33:56.31213-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-1wk6","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ux1f","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:37:38.635599-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
